examples.description: Include comments, hidden text, and slide masters that are off by default.

examples.title: Limit an Excel workbook to selected sheets
examples.description: Skip data and lookup sheets during extraction.

examples.title: Extract only specific PowerPoint slides
examples.description: Translate slides 1, 2, and 5 and skip the rest.

entries.description: Read and write plain text split into paragraphs at blank lines.

doc.overview:
Paragraph plain text treats a `.txt` document as a sequence of
paragraphs separated by blank lines (a double newline). Each
paragraph becomes one translatable block; the blank-line separators
between paragraphs are preserved as non-translatable structure.

This is the right reader when the natural translation unit is a whole
paragraph rather than a single line — prose, descriptions, or release
notes where a sentence may wrap across several physical lines. Line
breaks inside a paragraph are kept in the block text, so internal
wrapping survives the round-trip.
limitations: No sub-paragraph segmentation; the entire paragraph is a single block. Apply a segmentation tool downstream if sentence-level units are needed.
There are no configurable parameters; the paragraph boundary is always the blank line.
processingNotes: Paragraphs are split on a blank line (double newline); CRLF and LF endings are both recognised.
With a skeleton store active, original line endings and blank-line separators are preserved for byte-exact round-trips; leading and trailing blank lines round-trip as skeleton.

examples.title: Translate prose paragraphs
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to extract each
blank-line-separated paragraph as one block.

entries.description: Scrape text content from PDF files for word counts and leverage analysis.

doc.overview:
PDF (Portable Document Format) is a page-description format. neokapi's PDF
reader is a content-stream walker: it locates the text-showing operators in
each page's content streams (inflating FlateDecode segments first) and
collects the literal and hex strings they draw. The literal-string scanner
honours the PDF string grammar, including escape sequences and balanced
nested parentheses. Every text run inside a page becomes one Block joined
with single spaces; there is no paragraph segmentation.

PDF support is intended for scraping text — word counts, leverage analysis,
and quick content review — not for layout-preserving round-trips. The reader
does not consult font dictionaries, so strings encoded as glyph indices
through a ToUnicode CMap (some CID/Type0 fonts) are filtered out rather than
surfaced as garbage. PDF is **read-only**: it registers no writer, so it
cannot be edited or merged back — `kapi formats list` reports
`has_writer: false`, and tools such as `ksed` fail cleanly rather than
replacing the document with non-PDF plain text. This format has no
configurable JSON schema in this reference.
limitations: Read-only — PDF registers no writer, so it cannot be edited or round-tripped back to PDF.
Complex structure such as tables and multi-level lists is not reconstructed; there is no paragraph segmentation.
Text encoded as glyph indices through a ToUnicode CMap (some CID/Type0 fonts) is dropped rather than surfaced.
processingNotes: Reads text from content streams via the text-showing operators (`Tj`, `TJ`, `'`, `"`), inflating FlateDecode segments.
Each page's text runs are joined with single spaces into one Block; no paragraph segmentation is applied.
Read-only — PDF registers no writer, so editing or merging is rejected.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from PHP source files, optionally scoped by inline localization directives.

schema.title: PHP Content Filter
schema.description: Extracts translatable strings from PHP source files

extractOutsideDirectives.title: Extract outside the scope of the directives
extractOutsideDirectives.description: Extract translatable strings found outside directive-controlled regions.

useDirectives.title: Use localization directives
useDirectives.description: Honor //okapi: skip/text directives in PHP source to control extraction scope.

doc.overview:
The **PHP Content** format reads PHP source files and extracts the
translatable text they contain — the text outside PHP code, and text within
regions marked by localization directives. It is intended for PHP templates
and content files where prose is interleaved with code.

Extraction can be scoped with `//okapi:` directives embedded as comments in
the source, which turn extraction on (`text`) and off (`skip`) for regions
of the file. By default these directives are honored, and text outside the
directive-controlled regions is also extracted.

extractOutsideDirectives.help:
When enabled (the default), translatable text found outside the regions
governed by directives is still extracted. Disable it to extract only
the regions explicitly marked by directives.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when directives are honored

useDirectives.help:
When enabled (the default), `//okapi:` directives in the PHP source —
`skip` and `text` — control which regions of the file are extracted.
Disable it to ignore the directives entirely.

limitations: Directive scoping relies on `//okapi:` comments in the source; files without them are extracted according to `extractOutsideDirectives`.
processingNotes: Translatable text becomes blocks; PHP code and structure form the non-translatable skeleton.

examples.title: Only directive-marked regions
examples.description: Extract just the regions a translator has marked with directives.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from plain `.txt` files, one block per line or per paragraph.

schema.title: Plain Text Format
schema.description: Configuration for the plain text format reader/writer

segmentByLine.description: If true, each line becomes a separate block. If false, paragraphs (separated by blank lines) are blocks.

doc.overview:
The plain text format reads generic `.txt` documents and emits the
text content as translatable blocks. It is the fallback for files that
carry no markup or structure of their own — release notes, prompts,
word lists, or any free-form text that needs translating.

By default the reader segments by line: each non-empty line becomes one
block, and blank lines and the original line endings (LF, CRLF, or bare
CR) are preserved as skeleton so the document round-trips after merge.
Switching to paragraph mode groups runs of lines separated by blank
lines into a single block, which suits prose where sentences span
several wrapped lines. This mirrors Okapi's plain-text filter.

segmentByLine.description: Treat each line as a block, rather than each paragraph.
segmentByLine.help:
When enabled (the default), every non-empty line of the input
becomes its own block. Blank lines and line terminators are kept as
skeleton and are not extracted.

When disabled, the reader groups consecutive lines into paragraphs —
a block ends at a blank line. Use paragraph mode for prose that wraps
across multiple physical lines, where a sentence should stay in one
block rather than being split per line.

processingNotes: LF, CRLF, and bare CR are all recognised as line terminators and preserved.
Blank lines emit a non-translatable data part; they are never extracted as blocks.

examples.title: Paragraph mode
examples.description: Group wrapped lines into paragraph blocks instead of one block per line.

entries.description: Read and write GNU gettext PO and POT catalogues, extracting each message as a translatable block.

schema.title: PO Format (GNU Gettext)
schema.description: Configuration for the PO/POT format reader/writer

allowEmptyOutputTarget.title: Allow Empty Target Output
allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Allow empty target in output when no translation is available

bilingualMode.title: Bilingual Mode
bilingualMode.description: In bilingual mode (default), msgid is the source text and msgstr is the target. In monolingual mode, msgid is treated as an ID.

codeFinderRules.title: Code Finder Rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

preserveUntranslated.title: Preserve Untranslated Entries
preserveUntranslated.description: If true, entries with empty msgstr are emitted as blocks. If false, they are skipped.

useCodeFinder.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection within PO message text

wrapContent.title: Wrap Long Content Lines
wrapContent.description: Wrap long content lines in output at approximately 80 characters

doc.overview:
PO (Portable Object) is the GNU gettext catalogue format. A `.po` file
pairs `msgid` source strings with `msgstr` translations, optionally
disambiguated by `msgctxt`, with plural forms via `msgid_plural` and
`msgstr[N]`, translator and extracted comments, source references, and
`#, fuzzy` flags. `.pot` templates carry the same structure with empty
translations.

The reader emits one translatable block per message: in the default
bilingual mode the `msgid` is the source text and the `msgstr` is the
target. In monolingual mode the `msgid` is treated purely as an
identifier and the `msgstr` provides the source text instead — the
shape used by catalogues that ship message IDs rather than English
strings. Comments, context, and references are preserved on the block
and written back. This mirrors Okapi's PO filter.

allowEmptyOutputTarget.description: Write an empty target when no translation is available.
allowEmptyOutputTarget.help:
A writer-side option. When enabled, a message with no available
translation is written with an empty `msgstr` rather than being
dropped or filled with the source. When disabled (the default),
empty targets are not emitted.

bilingualMode.description: Treat msgid as the source text and msgstr as the target.
bilingualMode.help:
When enabled (the default), each message is read bilingually: the
`msgid` is the source text and the `msgstr` is the target
translation. When disabled (monolingual mode), the `msgid` is used
only as an identifier and the `msgstr` is taken as the source text —
the layout used by catalogues whose message IDs are symbolic rather
than English.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in message text.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within a translatable
block is protected as an inline code rather than translated. Used to
shield gettext placeholders or format specifiers. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

preserveUntranslated.description: Emit blocks for messages that have an empty msgstr.
preserveUntranslated.help:
Controls whether messages with an empty `msgstr` (untranslated
entries) are emitted as blocks. When enabled (the default), they are
kept so the full catalogue is visible for translation. When
disabled, untranslated entries are skipped.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside message text using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders,
variables, format specifiers) within the message text, using the
patterns in `codeFinderRules`. When disabled (the default), no
pattern-based inline codes are extracted.

wrapContent.description: Wrap long content lines in the output catalogue.
wrapContent.help:
A writer-side option. When enabled (the default), long `msgid` and
`msgstr` values are wrapped across multiple lines at roughly 80
characters, matching conventional gettext formatting. When disabled,
each value is written on a single line. The reader ignores this
setting.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per message; `msgctxt` is stored on the block context, and comments and references are preserved.
Plural forms (`msgid_plural` / `msgstr[N]`) and `#, fuzzy` flags are recognised and round-tripped.

examples.title: Monolingual catalogue
examples.description: Treat msgid as an identifier and msgstr as the source text.

examples.title: Skip untranslated entries
examples.description: Extract only messages that already carry a translation.

entries.description: Read and write Qt Linguist TS (.ts) files, the bilingual XML used by Qt's lupdate and lrelease.

doc.overview:
Qt Linguist TS is the bilingual XML format used by Qt's `lupdate` and
`lrelease` tools to carry UI translations. A `<TS>` document groups
one or more `<context>` elements; each context groups `<message>`
entries with a `<source>` and a `<translation>`. Translation state is
encoded on the `<translation>` element's `type` attribute — no type
means approved, `unfinished` means pending review, and `obsolete`
means the entry is unused. Plural messages (`numerus="yes"`) carry one
`<numerusform>` per locale plural form. Inline non-XML bytes appear as
`<byte value="…"/>` elements.

The reader extracts each message's source as a translatable block,
matched with its existing translation, and preserves the context and
message structure for the writer. The format is detected by sniffing
for a `<TS>` … `</TS>` document rather than by the `.ts` extension,
which collides with TypeScript.
limitations: The format is not auto-detected by the `.ts` file extension (it conflicts with TypeScript); detection relies on sniffing the `<TS>` root element or an explicit format declaration.
Non-XML bytes (`<byte value="…"/>` elements) are surfaced as inline placeholder runs and preserved through exchange; they are not decoded to their raw character equivalents on read.
There are no configurable parameters; behaviour follows the default contract.
processingNotes: One translatable block per `<message>`; context and message structure round-trip as skeleton.
Plural messages carry one `<numerusform>` per locale plural form.
Translation state (approved / unfinished / obsolete) is read from the `<translation>` `type` attribute.

examples.title: Translate a Qt UI catalogue
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to extract each
`<message>` source and merge translations back into the `.ts`
document.

entries.description: Extract translatable strings from arbitrary text formats using configurable regular-expression rules.

doc.overview:
The regex format is a configurable extractor for text formats that
have no dedicated reader — Mac `.strings`, INI files, StringInfo, and
similar key/value or delimited text. You supply a list of rules; each
rule is a regular expression with capture groups that identify the
translatable source text and, optionally, an identifier and a note.

Rules are applied across the input left to right. Where rules would
overlap, the earliest match wins. Each match produces one translatable
block whose source is the configured capture group; the text on either
side of the captured value is preserved verbatim so the writer can
rebuild the line by assembling prefix, translated value, and suffix.
Content that no rule matches is preserved as non-translatable
structure. With no rules configured, nothing is extracted and the
whole input is preserved.

Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a
`.kapi` project. The keys are: `rules` — a list of
`{ pattern, sourceGroup, idGroup, noteGroup }` records, where
`sourceGroup` is the 1-based capture group holding the source text
(required, must be at least 1), `idGroup` names the block
(`0` auto-generates IDs), and `noteGroup` captures a translator note
(`0` for none); `escapeType` — how to decode escapes inside the
extracted text, one of `none` (the default), `backslash` (`\"`, `\\`,
`\n`, `\t`, …), or `doublechar` (a character escaped by doubling it);
and `escapeChar` — the character used for `doublechar` mode (default
`"`).
limitations: Patterns use Go's RE2 regular-expression syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookarounds.
Where matches overlap, only the first match is kept; later overlapping matches are discarded.
Escape decoding (`escapeType`) applies to the extracted source text, which can differ from how a delimiter-scanning filter would handle the same escapes.
processingNotes: One block per rule match; non-matching spans become non-translatable Data so the output round-trips.
The raw text surrounding the captured source group is stored on the block so the writer reassembles output by `prefix + value + suffix`.
The optional `idGroup` and `noteGroup` captures populate the block name and a translator note respectively.

examples.title: Extract Mac .strings entries
examples.description:
A single rule capturing the value of a `"key" = "value";` line.
Group 1 is the source text; group 2 supplies the block ID.
examples.config:
format:
  name: regex
  config:
    rules:
      - pattern: '"([^"]*)"\s*=\s*"([^"]*)";'
        sourceGroup: 2
        idGroup: 1
    escapeType: backslash

entries.description: Extract translatable text from Rich Text Format documents.

doc.overview:
RTF (Rich Text Format) is Microsoft's text-based document interchange
format, structured as a stream of control words and groups. It is also the
carrier for Trados-tagged bilingual RTF. neokapi walks the RTF token stream,
extracts text from paragraph runs as translatable Blocks, and skips
non-translatable destinations — font, colour, and stylesheet tables,
document info, pictures, and fields.

neokapi reads and writes RTF. It decodes the common RTF escapes on read —
`\uN` Unicode escapes, `\'HH` hex escapes, and special-character control
words such as `\par`, `\tab`, `\emdash`, and the smart-quote words — and
preserves non-translatable structure as skeleton. By default it does not
extract header and footer text, annotation (comment) text, or bookmark text;
the upstream Okapi RTF filter exposes no parameters, and these neokapi-side
extraction toggles are not surfaced as a configurable JSON schema in this
reference.
limitations: Header/footer, annotation (comment), and bookmark text are not extracted by default.
The neokapi extraction toggles have no upstream Okapi parameter and are not surfaced as configurable schema properties in this reference.
processingNotes: Translatable text comes from paragraph runs; control tables, info, pictures, and fields are skipped.
Decodes `\uN` Unicode escapes, `\'HH` hex escapes, and special-character control words (`\par`, `\tab`, `\emdash`, smart quotes).
Non-translatable destinations are preserved as skeleton for round-trips.

entries.description: Read and write plain text where a trailing backslash splices the next line onto the current one.

doc.overview:
Spliced-lines plain text is a line-oriented text format in which a
trailing backslash (`\`) at the end of a physical line continues the
logical line onto the next one. The reader joins adjacent physical
lines wherever the previous line ends in `\`, and emits one
translatable block per logical line. The splice marker is consumed;
the line break between spliced lines is kept inside the block text.

Use it for line-continuation conventions common in configuration and
scripting files, where a long value is wrapped across several
physical lines with a backslash. Lines without a trailing backslash
stand on their own as one block each.
limitations: The splice marker is fixed to the backslash character; it is not configurable.
Blank or whitespace-only logical lines are treated as non-translatable structure (skeleton), not extracted.
processingNotes: One block per logical line; spliced physical lines are joined with their original line breaks preserved in the block text.
A document that ends mid-continuation (a final line still ending in `\`) round-trips the trailing backslash, matching Okapi's `SplicedLinesFilter` behaviour.
With a skeleton store active, line endings and continuation markers are tracked so output is byte-exact.

examples.title: Extract continuation-joined lines
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to join
backslash-continued lines into single blocks.

entries.description: Read and write SubRip (.srt) subtitle files, extracting each cue's text for translation.

schema.title: SRT Subtitles
schema.description: SubRip subtitle format — no configurable parameters

doc.overview:
SubRip (`.srt`) is the line-oriented subtitle format used by most
video players. A file is a sequence of cues, each made of a sequence
number, a timecode range (`00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,000`), and one
or more lines of subtitle text, separated by a blank line.

The reader extracts each cue's subtitle text as a translatable block
and keeps the sequence number and timecode as block properties so the
writer can reconstruct the cue. Sequence numbers, timecodes, and the
blank-line separators are non-translatable structure: they are
carried in the skeleton and round-trip unchanged. Multi-line cues
preserve their internal line breaks in the block text, so a wrapped
caption stays wrapped.
limitations: Timecodes are preserved verbatim, not adjusted; retiming for a longer or shorter translation is out of scope.
SubRip styling tags inside cue text (for example `…` or `{\an8}`) are carried as part of the block text rather than parsed into inline codes.
processingNotes: One translatable block per cue; the sequence number and timecode ride along as block properties (`sequence`, `timecode`).
With a skeleton store active, sequence numbers, timecodes, and separators are stored as skeleton text for byte-exact round-trips, including the original LF / CRLF line endings.

examples.title: Translate subtitles
examples.description:
SubRip needs no configuration. Declare the format on a collection
to extract every cue's text; the cue numbering and timecodes are
preserved automatically.

entries.description: Extract translatable text from TeX and LaTeX documents.

doc.overview:
TeX/LaTeX is a markup-based typesetting language. neokapi reads the LaTeX2e
document syntax — preamble, body, command sequences, environments, math
mode, and comments — and extracts the prose that should be translated while
leaving the command structure intact.

neokapi reads and writes TeX/LaTeX. Body paragraphs inside
`\begin{document}` … `\end{document}` become translatable Blocks, one per
paragraph (paragraphs are separated by blank lines; a soft line break inside
a paragraph collapses to a space). Visible-text header commands such as
`\title`, `\author`, and `\date`, and paragraph-level commands such as
`\section`, `\chapter`, and `\caption`, are translatable. Inline formatting
commands such as `\textbf`, `\emph`, and `\footnote` fold into the
surrounding paragraph. Structural commands (`\label`, `\ref`, `\cite`,
`\usepackage`, `\documentclass`), comments, math (inline and environment),
and verbatim/listing environments are non-translatable and preserved as
skeleton. This format has no configurable JSON schema in this reference.
limitations: Math (inline and environment) and verbatim/listing environments are not extracted.
Comments and structural commands are non-translatable.
processingNotes: Body paragraphs inside the document environment become one translatable Block each; blank lines separate paragraphs.
Header and sectioning commands (`\title`, `\section`, `\caption`, …) are translatable; inline commands fold into the surrounding paragraph.
Structural commands, comments, math, and verbatim environments are preserved as skeleton for round-trips.

entries.description: Read and write TMX translation-memory exchange files, one block per translation unit.

schema.title: TMX (Translation Memory eXchange)
schema.description: Configuration for the native TMX translation memory format reader/writer

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

exitOnInvalid.title: Exit on invalid TUs
exitOnInvalid.description: Stop processing when encountering invalid TUs. When false, invalid TUs are skipped.

processAllTargets.title: Read all target entries
processAllTargets.description: Read all target language TUVs from each TU. When false, only the first target is read.

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection in translatable text

doc.overview:
TMX (Translation Memory eXchange) is the XML interchange format for
translation memories. A TMX document holds a header declaring the
source language (`srclang`) and a body of `<tu>` translation units;
each unit carries one `<tuv>` translation-unit variant per language,
with optional unit- and header-level `<note>` and `<prop>` metadata.

The reader emits one translatable block per `<tu>`: the source segment
matches the header `srclang` and a target segment is produced for each
other-language `<tuv>`. The header attributes and any header-level note
or property are surfaced as a single non-translatable data part ahead
of the blocks, while unit-level notes and properties attach to the
block. This makes TMX usable both as an input for review or alignment
and as an output for exporting a memory. It mirrors Okapi's TMX filter.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in segment text.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within a translatable
block is protected as an inline code rather than translated. Used to
shield placeholders or markup carried inside TM segments. Only takes
effect when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

escapeGT.description: Escape the greater-than character as `>` in output XML.
escapeGT.help:
A writer-side option. When enabled, the `>` character is written as
the `>` entity. When disabled (the default), `>` is written
literally, which is valid XML outside of a CDATA close sequence.
Enable it for stricter downstream tools that expect `>` escaped.

exitOnInvalid.description: Stop processing when an invalid translation unit is encountered.
exitOnInvalid.help:
Governs error handling for malformed `<tu>` elements. When disabled
(the default), invalid units are skipped silently and parsing
continues. When enabled, the first invalid unit aborts processing —
use it to fail fast on corrupt or non-conformant input.

processAllTargets.description: Read every target-language variant in each translation unit.
processAllTargets.help:
When enabled (the default), all non-source `<tuv>` variants of a
`<tu>` are read, producing a target segment for each language. When
disabled, only the first non-source variant is read and the rest are
skipped — useful when a multilingual memory should be reduced to a
single target language.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside segment text using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes (placeholders,
variables, tags) within translatable text, using the patterns in
`codeFinderRules`. When disabled (the default), no pattern-based
inline codes are extracted.

processingNotes: The source segment matches the header `srclang`; one target segment is produced per other-language `<tuv>`.
Header attributes and header-level notes and properties are emitted as a single non-translatable data part ahead of the blocks; unit-level notes and properties attach to the block.

examples.title: Read only the first target
examples.description: Reduce a multilingual memory to a single target language.

examples.title: Fail fast on invalid units
examples.description: Abort processing when a malformed translation unit is found.

entries.description: Read and write Trados TagEditor TTX bilingual documents.

doc.overview:
TTX is the XML-based bilingual format produced by Trados TagEditor and read
by several other translation tools. There is no official public TTX
specification. The format stores source and target text as translation
units, carries existing segmentation, and records match percentages and
translation annotations.

neokapi reads and writes TTX. It walks the XML once, collects segment text
inside the `<Tu>`/`<Tuv>` translation-unit elements, and emits one Block per
translation unit — the first translation-unit variant as the source and the
second as the target. The surrounding XML is preserved as skeleton. By
default the reader auto-detects segmentation (extracting existing segments
when present, otherwise all text), and the writer does not escape
greater-than characters. These choices track the upstream Okapi TTX filter;
the native reader does not expose them as a configurable JSON schema in this
reference.
limitations: There is no official public TTX specification; behaviour follows the conventions Trados tools write.
Segmentation mode and output-escaping toggles match the upstream Okapi TTX filter but are not surfaced as configurable schema properties in this reference.
processingNotes: One Block per `<Tu>`; the first `<Tuv>` becomes the source and the second the target.
Segmentation is auto-detected by default — existing segments are extracted when present, otherwise all text.
The surrounding XML is preserved as skeleton for round-trips.

entries.description: Read and write TXML bilingual documents (the Wordfast Pro XML format).

doc.overview:
TXML is the XML-based bilingual format used by Wordfast Pro and supported by
several other tools. There is no official public TXML specification. A TXML
document has a top-level `<txml>` root carrying `locale` and `targetlocale`
attributes, optional `<skeleton>` wrappers holding original markup, and
`<translatable>` blocks whose `<segment>` children each hold a
`<source>`/`<target>` pair.

neokapi reads and writes TXML. Each `<translatable>` element becomes one
Block, with one segment per `<segment>` child, preserving existing
translations on read. The surrounding markup is kept as skeleton. By default
the writer permits empty target segments — emitting an empty target element
for an untranslated segment rather than omitting it — matching the upstream
Okapi TXML filter. The native reader applies this default but does not
expose it as a configurable JSON schema in this reference.
limitations: There is no official public TXML specification; behaviour follows the schema the upstream Okapi filter implements.
The empty-target output toggle matches the upstream Okapi TXML filter but is not surfaced as a configurable schema property in this reference.
processingNotes: One Block per `<translatable>` element, with one segment per `<segment>` child.
Existing `<source>`/`<target>` translations are preserved on read; surrounding markup is kept as skeleton.
By default an untranslated segment writes an empty target element rather than omitting it.

entries.description: Read and write Okapi's TransTable v1 tab-separated bilingual exchange table.

doc.overview:
TransTable v1 is the tab-separated bilingual exchange table that
Okapi's generic writer produces for its "Translation Table" output.
The first line is a header declaring the signature (`TransTableV1`)
and the source and target locales as three tab-separated cells. Each
following line is a context crumb (`okpCtx:tu=<id>`) followed by a
source cell and an optional target cell.

The reader extracts one translatable block per text unit, pairing the
source and target cells. Cells may be wrapped in double quotes, which
are stripped on read; the literal sequences `\t` and `\n` inside a
cell are unescaped to tab and newline. Whitespace-only lines are
skipped. A crumb may carry a segment suffix (`:s=<seg-id>`); rows that
share the same `tu=<id>` and carry segment suffixes are merged into a
single segmented text unit. A missing or malformed header aborts the
read.

Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a
`.kapi` project. The one key is `allowSegments` (default true): when
enabled, `:s=<seg-id>` suffixes are honoured and rows sharing a
`tu=<id>` merge into one segmented unit; when disabled, every row is
its own text unit regardless of any segment suffix. It mirrors
Okapi's `allowSegments` parameter.
limitations: The header must be a valid `TransTableV1` signature line; otherwise the read fails.
Only the v1 table shape is supported.
processingNotes: One block per text unit; source and target cells pair into source and target segments.
Surrounding double quotes are stripped and `\t` / `\n` escapes are decoded on read.
With `allowSegments` enabled, rows sharing a `tu=<id>` and carrying `:s=<seg-id>` suffixes merge into one segmented block.

examples.title: Read a bilingual table without segment grouping
examples.description:
Disable segment grouping so each row becomes its own text unit,
regardless of any `:s=<seg-id>` suffix on the crumb.
examples.config:
format:
  name: transtable
  config:
    allowSegments: false

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable cells from tab-separated value files, with column-based selection.

schema.title: CSV Format
schema.description: Configuration for the CSV/TSV format reader/writer

codeFinderRules.title: Code Finder Rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

columnNamesRow.title: Column Names Row
columnNamesRow.description: 1-based row number containing column names. 0 means auto-detect.

commentColumns.title: Comment Columns
commentColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that contain comments or notes

hasHeader.title: Has Header Row
hasHeader.description: If true, the first row is treated as headers (non-translatable)

keyColumns.title: Key Columns
keyColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) that provide the block ID (source ID / record ID)

separator.title: Field Delimiter
separator.description: Field delimiter character (comma for CSV, tab for TSV)

textQualifier.title: Text Qualifier
textQualifier.description: Character used to quote field values containing delimiters or newlines

translatableColumns.title: Translatable Columns
translatableColumns.description: Column indices (0-based) to extract as translatable content. If empty, all columns are translatable.

trimValues.title: Trim Values
trimValues.description: If true, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from cell values

useCodeFinder.title: Enable Inline Code Detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection within cell values

valuesStartRow.title: Values Start Row
valuesStartRow.description: 1-based row number where data values begin. 0 means auto-detect.

doc.overview:
The **TSV** format reads tab-separated value files, extracts selected
columns as translatable blocks, and writes the translations back into the
same rows. It shares its implementation with the CSV format, configured with
a tab delimiter, so the same column-selection, key, comment, and inline-code
options apply.

By default the first row is treated as a header and every data column is
translatable. You can restrict extraction to specific columns, designate key
columns that supply each block's ID, mark comment columns whose values
become notes, and adjust the delimiter, quote character, and row offsets.

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within cells.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

columnNamesRow.help:
The 1-based row that holds the column names. `0` means auto-detect: row
1 is used when `hasHeader` is enabled.

commentColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns holding comments or notes.
commentColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values are recorded as notes on the
row's translatable blocks rather than extracted as content.

hasHeader.description: Treat the first row as a non-translatable header.
hasHeader.help:
When enabled (the default), the first row is treated as column headers
and is not extracted. Disable it for files whose data begins on the
first row.

keyColumns.description: 0-based indices of columns providing the block ID.
keyColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose values supply each block's ID
(source/record identifier). When several are given, their values are
joined with `.`. When empty, row-based IDs are used.
keyColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — row-based IDs)
examples: `[0]` — use the first column as the record ID

separator.help:
The character separating fields — a tab for TSV. The same setting drives
the CSV variant with a comma.
separator.values: Single character (default `,`; use a tab for TSV)

textQualifier.description: Quote character for fields with delimiters or newlines.
textQualifier.help:
The character used to quote field values that contain the delimiter, a
newline, or the quote character itself.

translatableColumns.help:
The 0-based column indices whose cells are extracted as translatable
content. When empty, every column is treated as translatable.
translatableColumns.values: Array of 0-based integers (default empty — all columns)
examples: `[1, 2]` — extract the second and third columns

trimValues.description: Strip surrounding whitespace from cell values.
trimValues.help:
When enabled, leading and trailing whitespace is removed from each cell
value before extraction.

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes within cell values. The
patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

valuesStartRow.help:
The 1-based row from which data values are read. `0` means auto-detect:
the row after the header, or row 1 when there is no header.

limitations: Column selection is index-based; columns are not addressed by header name.
TSV and CSV share one schema; the only intrinsic difference is the tab delimiter.
processingNotes: Each selected cell becomes a translatable block; row structure and the tab delimiter form the non-translatable skeleton.
Key columns supply IDs and comment columns supply notes; they are not themselves extracted as translatable content.

examples.title: Translate one column, keyed by another
examples.description: Extract column 2, using column 0 as the record ID.

examples.title: Headerless file
examples.description: Read a tab-separated file whose data begins on the first row.

entries.description: Read and write W3C Timed Text Markup Language subtitle and caption files.

schema.title: TTML Filter
schema.description: Timed Text Markup Language (W3C standard) subtitle format

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK
cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line for CJK languages. Set to 0 to disable.

escapeBR.title: Escape line breaks
escapeBR.description:
When true,
elements are removed and text is joined with spaces; when false,
is preserved as literal text.

maxCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line
maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line in output. Set to 0 to disable.

maxLinesPerCaption.title: Max lines per caption
maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of lines per caption in output. Set to 0 to disable.

mergeAdjacentCaptions.title: Merge adjacent captions
mergeAdjacentCaptions.description:
Merge adjacent

elements ending with trailing punctuation into one block.

splitWords.title: Split words at character limit
splitWords.description: Split words so that they don't go over the character limit per line.

doc.overview:
TTML (Timed Text Markup Language) is the W3C XML standard for timed text — subtitles and captions — also distributed as `.dfxp`. A TTML document carries its captions inside a ``/`
`/`

` hierarchy in the `http://www.w3.org/ns/ttml` namespace, with timing and positioning expressed as attributes (`begin`, `end`, `dur`, `region`, `textAlign`). The reader walks the `

` hierarchy and emits one translatable block per non-empty `

`. The timing and positioning attributes are kept on the block's properties and written back unchanged. Inline styling such as `` is unwrapped into the block's text; `
` handling is controlled by `escapeBR`. This mirrors Okapi's TTML filter, with the native default of one block per `

`.

cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line for CJK languages.
cjkCharsPerLine.help:
A separate per-line character cap for CJK text, where each character
is visually wider. `0` (the default) falls back to `maxCharsPerLine`.
Output-side; does not affect extraction. Accepted for parity with
Okapi's TTML writer; the native writer does not currently enforce it.

dependsOn.condition: used as the fallback when cjkCharsPerLine is 0

escapeBR.description:
Control whether `
` line breaks are collapsed or kept as literal text.
escapeBR.help:
Governs how `
` elements inside a caption are extracted. When enabled (the default), `
` is removed and the surrounding text is joined with a single space, so the block holds one flowing line of text. When disabled, `
` is preserved as the literal string `
` inside the block's source text, keeping the in-caption line breaks visible to the translator.

maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line of caption text on output.
maxCharsPerLine.help:
Caps the line length of written caption text. `0` (the default)
means no limit. This is an output-side parameter modelled on Okapi's
TTML writer; it does not affect extraction, and the native writer
accepts it for configuration parity without currently rewrapping
caption text.

maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of text lines per caption on output.
maxLinesPerCaption.help:
Caps how many lines a single caption may occupy. `0` (the default)
means no limit. Output-side; does not affect extraction. Accepted
for parity with Okapi's TTML writer; the native writer does not
currently enforce it.

mergeAdjacentCaptions.description: Merge consecutive captions that continue a sentence into one block.
mergeAdjacentCaptions.help:
When enabled, an adjacent `

` caption is merged into the previous block when the previous text ends with a continuation punctuation mark (a comma or semicolon). This produces fewer, sentence-level blocks. When disabled (the default), every non-empty `

` becomes its own block.

splitWords.description: Allow splitting words to keep within the character limit.
splitWords.help:
When enabled, words may be broken across lines to honour the
per-line character cap; when disabled, line breaks fall on word
boundaries only. Takes effect only alongside a non-zero character
limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's TTML writer; the native
writer does not currently rewrap text.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a character limit is set

limitations: The output line-wrapping parameters (maxCharsPerLine, maxLinesPerCaption, cjkCharsPerLine, splitWords) are accepted for parity with Okapi but are not yet enforced by the native writer; caption text is written as supplied.
processingNotes:
One block is emitted per non-empty `

`; timing and positioning attributes (begin, end, dur, region, textAlign) are preserved on block properties.
Inline styling such as `<span>` is unwrapped into the block's source text.

examples.title: Keep in-caption line breaks
examples.description:
Preserve `
` as literal text so the translator can see line breaks.

examples.title: Merge sentence-spanning captions
examples.description: Combine adjacent captions that continue a sentence into one block.

entries.description: Read and write verse-numbered plain text (scripture, poetry, lyrics), one block per verse.

doc.overview:
Versified text is a line-oriented format for verse-numbered content
such as scripture, poetry, and lyrics. Each line opens with a verse
marker — either USFM-style (`\v 1` or `\v1`) or a plain numeric
prefix (`1.` or `1 `). The marker is consumed and the rest of the
line becomes one translatable block; the verse number is kept in the
block's `verse` property so the writer can re-emit the original
numbering.

Lines with no recognised marker are still extracted as blocks (with
an empty `verse` property), so non-numbered material is not lost.
Blank lines act as stanza breaks: they are preserved as
non-translatable structure and never translated.
limitations: Recognised markers are USFM `\v` (with or without a space) and a leading positive integer followed by `.` or a space; other numbering schemes are treated as ordinary lines.
This is a native-only format; the okapi-bridge daemon ships no equivalent filter.
processingNotes: One block per line; the verse number rides along in the block's `verse` property and the block name follows `verse.<N>`.
Blank lines between verses emit a stanza-break Data part and round-trip as skeleton.
With a skeleton store active, the verse marker prefix and line endings are stored as skeleton text for byte-exact round-trips.

examples.title: Translate verse-numbered text
examples.description:
No configuration is required. Declare the format to extract one
block per verse while preserving verse numbers and stanza breaks.

entries.description: Read and write Vignette CMS export/import XML (the output of the vgnexport tool).

doc.overview:
This format reads and writes the export/import XML produced by the Vignette content management system's `vgnexport` tool. A document contains one or more `` blocks, each holding a set of `` payloads for one content item in one locale. The reader walks every instance and extracts the payloads whose attribute names are listed in the parts configuration; the remaining XML structure is preserved. By default the reader works bilingually: source and target instances are paired through the value of the source-id attribute, and only paired instances reach the translatable stream. In monolingual mode every instance is extracted independently. Encoding is forced to UTF-8 on read and write, matching the upstream filter. A payload may itself contain HTML; for attributes configured with the `okf_html` sub-filter the reader decodes HTML entities and strips an outer `

` wrapper, while every other configuration treats the payload as a single literal block. Configuration is supplied on the collection's `format:` block in a `.kapi` project, with keys that mirror the Okapi parameter names: `partsNames` — a comma-separated list of attribute names to extract (defaults to the standard SMC attribute set); `partsConfigurations` — a comma-separated list of sub-filter ids paired by index with `partsNames`, where `okf_html` decodes HTML and any other value (including `default`) means no sub-filtering; `sourceId` — the attribute name holding the source-id used to pair instances (default `SOURCE_ID`); `localeId` — the attribute name holding the locale identifier (default `LOCALE_ID`); `monolingual` — when true, extract every instance independently instead of pairing; and `useCDATA` — when true, wrap written payloads in a CDATA section (write-side only).
limitations: In bilingual mode (the default), only instances that pair through the source-id attribute are extracted; unpaired instances are skipped.
The parts configuration recognises only `okf_html` as a sub-filter; every other value, including `default`, is treated as no sub-filtering.
Input and output encoding are forced to UTF-8.
processingNotes: One block per extracted attribute payload; `partsNames` and `partsConfigurations` are paired by list index.
Source and target instances pair via the `sourceId` attribute value unless `monolingual` is enabled.
The `useCDATA` option affects only the writer; it wraps emitted payloads in a CDATA section and has no effect on reading.

examples.title: Extract specific attributes monolingually
examples.description:
Override the parts list and extract every instance independently,
decoding the body attribute as HTML.
examples.config:
format:
  name: vignette
  config:
    partsNames: "SMCCONTENT-TITLE, SMCCONTENT-BODY"
    partsConfigurations: "default, okf_html"
    monolingual: true

entries.description: Read and write WebVTT subtitle files, extracting one block per cue.

schema.title: VTT Filter
schema.description: Web Video Text Tracks (WebVTT) subtitle format

cjkCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line for CJK
cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line for CJK languages. Set to 0 to disable.

maxCharsPerLine.title: Max characters per line
maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum number of characters per line in output. Set to 0 to disable.

maxLinesPerCaption.title: Max lines per caption
maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of lines per caption in output. Set to 0 to disable.

splitWords.title: Split words at character limit
splitWords.description: Split words so that they don't go over the character limit per line.

doc.overview:
WebVTT (Web Video Text Tracks) is the W3C subtitle and caption format
used by HTML5 `<track>` elements. A `.vtt` file opens with a `WEBVTT`
header and then lists cues: an optional cue identifier, a timecode line
(`HH:MM:SS.mmm --> HH:MM:SS.mmm` plus optional positioning settings),
and one or more lines of cue text.

The reader emits the `WEBVTT` header as a non-translatable data part,
each cue identifier as its own data part, and one translatable block per
cue. The timecode (including any cue settings) is preserved on the
block's `timecode` property and written back verbatim, so the timing
survives the round-trip. Cues are kept separate — captions are not
merged by sentence — so each cue is translated independently.

cjkCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line for CJK languages.
cjkCharsPerLine.help:
A separate per-line character cap applied to CJK text, where each
character is visually wider. `0` (the default) falls back to
`maxCharsPerLine`. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer;
the native writer does not currently enforce it.

dependsOn.condition: used as the fallback when cjkCharsPerLine is 0

maxCharsPerLine.description: Maximum characters per line of cue text on output.
maxCharsPerLine.help:
Caps the line length of written cue text. `0` (the default) means no
limit. This is an output-side parameter modelled on Okapi's WebVTT
writer; the native writer accepts it for configuration parity but
does not currently rewrap cue text — lines are written as supplied
by the block.

maxLinesPerCaption.description: Maximum number of text lines per cue on output.
maxLinesPerCaption.help:
Caps how many lines a single cue may occupy. `0` (the default) means
no limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer; the native
writer does not currently enforce it.

splitWords.description: Allow splitting words to keep within the character limit.
splitWords.help:
When enabled, words may be broken across lines to honour the
per-line character cap; when disabled, line breaks fall on word
boundaries only. Takes effect only alongside a non-zero character
limit. Accepted for parity with Okapi's WebVTT writer; the native
writer does not currently rewrap text.

dependsOn.condition: only relevant when a character limit is set

limitations: The output line-wrapping parameters (maxCharsPerLine, maxLinesPerCaption, cjkCharsPerLine, splitWords) are accepted for parity with Okapi but are not yet enforced by the native writer; cue text is written as supplied.
Cues are never merged by sentence; each cue is a separate block.
processingNotes: The WEBVTT header is emitted as a data part; cue identifiers become their own data parts.
The cue timecode and its settings are preserved on the block `timecode` property and written back unchanged.

examples.title: Default extraction
examples.description: Read each cue as a translatable block with no output line limits.

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable text from wiki markup (DokuWiki or MediaWiki).

schema.title: Wiki Filter
schema.description: Configuration for the Wiki (MediaWiki/DokuWiki) format reader/writer

preserveWhitespace.title: Preserve Whitespace
preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in wiki markup instead of normalizing it.

variant.title: Wiki variant
variant.description: Wiki markup variant: mediawiki or dokuwiki. Default is dokuwiki to match the okf_wiki bridge contract.

doc.overview:
The **Wiki** format reads wiki markup, extracts prose as translatable
blocks, and writes the translations back while preserving the markup
structure — headings, lists, tables, links, and formatting. Inline markup is
exposed as inline codes so the surrounding syntax survives translation.

Two markup dialects are supported: DokuWiki and MediaWiki. The default is
DokuWiki, matching the upstream `okf_wiki` filter, whose documentation states
DokuWiki is the supported markup style; set `variant` to `mediawiki` to read
MediaWiki markup instead.

preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in the markup.
preserveWhitespace.help:
When enabled, original whitespace in the wiki markup is preserved rather
than normalized during extraction. By default whitespace is normalized.

limitations: The two dialects share one extraction engine; markup specific to one dialect is recognized only under the matching `variant`.
processingNotes: Headings, lists, tables, and formatting markers form the non-translatable skeleton; prose runs become translatable blocks.
Inline formatting and links within a run are exposed as inline codes so they survive translation.

examples.title: Read MediaWiki markup
examples.description: Parse a MediaWiki-flavoured document instead of the default DokuWiki.

examples.title: Preserve whitespace
examples.description: Keep original spacing in the markup instead of normalizing it.

entries.description: Read and write OASIS XLIFF 1.2 bilingual exchange documents.

schema.title: XLIFF 1.2
schema.description: Configuration for the native XLIFF 1.2 bilingual exchange format reader/writer

addAltTrans.title: Add alt-trans elements
addAltTrans.description: Allow addition of new alt-trans elements

addTargetLanguage.title: Add target-language attribute
addTargetLanguage.description: Add the target-language attribute to file elements if not already present

allowEmptyTargets.title: Allow empty targets
allowEmptyTargets.description: Allow target elements with empty content to be written

alwaysAddTargets.title: Add targets for monolingual XLIFF
alwaysAddTargets.description: Add target elements for monolingual XLIFF files that have no target in the source

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

editAltTrans.title: Edit alt-trans elements
editAltTrans.description: Allow editing of existing alt-trans elements

fallbackToID.title: Use trans-unit ID as name if no resname
fallbackToID.description: Use the trans-unit id attribute as the block name when no resname attribute is present

forceUniqueIds.title: Enforce unique trans-unit IDs
forceUniqueIds.description: Rewrite trans-unit IDs to enforce uniqueness across the file

ignoreInputSegmentation.title: Ignore input segmentation
ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Ignore seg-source/mrk segmentation from the input XLIFF and treat the full source as a single segment

includeExtensions.title: Include XLIFF extension elements
includeExtensions.description: Include non-standard namespace extension elements in extracted content

includeIts.title: Include ITS markup
includeIts.description: Include ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) markup in extracted content

overrideTargetLanguage.title: Override target language
overrideTargetLanguage.description: Override the target-language attribute in the output document with the pipeline target locale

preserveSpaceByDefault.title: Preserve whitespace by default
preserveSpaceByDefault.description: Treat all content as xml:space="preserve" unless explicitly overridden

targetStateValue.title: Target state value
targetStateValue.description: State value to set on target elements when useTranslationTargetState is enabled

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection in translatable text

useTranslationTargetState.title: Update target state attribute
useTranslationTargetState.description: Update the state attribute on target elements when writing translated XLIFF

doc.overview:
XLIFF 1.2 is the OASIS XML Localisation Interchange File Format — the
bilingual exchange format that translation tools and TMS platforms use
to move content between extraction and translation. A document groups
`<trans-unit>` elements under `<file>` containers, each unit holding a
`<source>` and an optional `<target>`, with inline markup represented
through paired (`bpt`/`ept`, `g`) or standalone (`x`, `ph`) code
elements and segmentation expressed via `<seg-source>` and
`<mrk mtype="seg">`.

The reader emits one block per translatable `<trans-unit>`, carrying
the source, any existing target, segmentation, alt-trans matches, and
unit metadata. The writer updates targets and, by default, the target
`state` attribute, so a round-tripped file reflects translation
progress. This mirrors Okapi's XLIFF 1.2 filter.

addAltTrans.description: Allow new alt-trans elements to be added.
addAltTrans.help:
When enabled, new `<alt-trans>` elements (for example, machine or
memory matches) may be written into units. When disabled (the
default), no new alt-trans elements are added.

addTargetLanguage.description: Add the target-language attribute to file elements if missing.
addTargetLanguage.help:
When enabled (the default), the `target-language` attribute is added
to `<file>` elements that lack one, using the pipeline target locale.
When disabled, the attribute is left as found in the input.

allowEmptyTargets.description: Allow target elements with empty content to be written.
allowEmptyTargets.help:
When enabled, `<target>` elements may be written with empty content.
When disabled (the default), empty targets are not emitted. Enable it
when downstream tooling expects a `<target>` placeholder to exist
even before translation.

alwaysAddTargets.description: Add target elements for monolingual XLIFF that has none.
alwaysAddTargets.help:
When enabled, `<target>` elements are added for monolingual XLIFF
inputs that contain only sources. When disabled (the default), no
targets are synthesised for source-only files.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in content.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within translatable text is
protected as an inline code rather than translated. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

editAltTrans.description: Allow editing of existing alt-trans elements.
editAltTrans.help:
When enabled, existing `<alt-trans>` (alternative translation /
match) elements may be modified on write. When disabled (the
default), they are preserved unchanged.

fallbackToID.description: Use the trans-unit id as the block name when no resname is present.
fallbackToID.help:
When enabled, a `<trans-unit>` with no `resname` attribute takes its
`id` attribute as the block name, so the block still has a
human-meaningful identifier. When disabled (the default), the block
name is left empty when `resname` is absent.

forceUniqueIds.description: Rewrite trans-unit ids to enforce uniqueness across the file.
forceUniqueIds.help:
When enabled, duplicate `<trans-unit>` `id` values are rewritten so
every id is unique across the file. When disabled (the default), ids
are left as-is. Enable it for inputs whose ids are not guaranteed
unique and where downstream tooling requires uniqueness.

ignoreInputSegmentation.description: Treat the full source as one segment, ignoring input segmentation.
ignoreInputSegmentation.help:
When enabled, `<seg-source>`/`<mrk mtype="seg">` segmentation in the
input is ignored and the whole `<source>` is treated as a single
segment. When disabled (the default), the existing segmentation is
honoured and each `<mrk>` segment becomes its own segment in the
block.

includeExtensions.description: Include non-standard namespace extension elements in extracted content.
includeExtensions.help:
When enabled (the default), elements from non-XLIFF namespaces
(vendor or tool extensions) found within content are kept in the
extracted block. When disabled, such extension elements are dropped
from the extracted content.

includeIts.description: Include ITS (Internationalization Tag Set) markup in extracted content.
includeIts.help:
When enabled (the default), ITS markup carried in the XLIFF (e.g.
translate, locale-filter, or terminology annotations) is preserved
in the extracted content. When disabled, ITS markup is omitted.

overrideTargetLanguage.description: Replace the target-language attribute with the pipeline target locale.
overrideTargetLanguage.help:
When enabled, the `target-language` attribute in the output is
overwritten with the pipeline's target locale even if the input
already declared one. When disabled (the default), an existing
`target-language` is preserved.

preserveSpaceByDefault.description: Treat all content as `xml:space="preserve"` unless overridden.
preserveSpaceByDefault.help:
When enabled, every element is treated as whitespace-preserving
regardless of an explicit `xml:space` attribute. When disabled (the
default), the reader honours per-element `xml:space` only, collapsing
insignificant whitespace elsewhere.

targetStateValue.description: The state value written to target elements.
targetStateValue.help:
The XLIFF state value applied to `<target>` elements when
`useTranslationTargetState` is enabled. The default is
`needs-translation`. Set it to a standard XLIFF 1.2 state such as
`translated`, `needs-review-translation`, or `final` to match your
workflow.

dependsOn.condition: applied only when target state updating is enabled

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside content using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes in translatable text,
using the patterns in `codeFinderRules`. This supplements the native
XLIFF inline elements. When disabled (the default), only the XLIFF
inline elements are recognised.

useTranslationTargetState.description: Update the target `state` attribute when writing translated XLIFF.
useTranslationTargetState.help:
When enabled (the default), the writer sets the `state` attribute on
`<target>` elements to reflect translation status, using
`targetStateValue`. When disabled, the existing `state` is left
untouched.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per translatable `<trans-unit>`; units marked `translate="no"` are passed through as non-translatable.
Inline markup (`bpt`/`ept`, `g`, `x`, `ph`) and `<seg-source>`/`<mrk mtype="seg">` segmentation are read into the block.

examples.title: Mark targets as translated
examples.description: Write a final target state instead of the default needs-translation.

examples.title: Flatten input segmentation
examples.description: Treat each source as one segment, ignoring seg-source markers.

entries.description: Read and write OASIS XLIFF 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 bilingual exchange documents.

schema.title: XLIFF 2.x
schema.description: Configuration for the native XLIFF 2.0/2.1/2.2 bilingual exchange format reader/writer

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

discardInvalidTargets.title: Discard invalid targets
discardInvalidTargets.description: Discard targets that fail validation rather than rejecting the entire file

forceUniqueIds.title: Ensure unique tag IDs
forceUniqueIds.description: Ensure inline tag IDs are unique within each unit

ignoreTagTypeMatch.title: Ignore tag type mismatch
ignoreTagTypeMatch.description: Ignore tag type mismatch between source and target segments

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline code detection in translatable text

version.title: XLIFF version
version.description: Target XLIFF 2.x version to emit. Empty means: preserve the input document version on roundtrip, otherwise default to 2.2.

writeOriginalData.title: Include original data in output
writeOriginalData.description: Output includes original data when available

doc.overview:
XLIFF 2.x is the modern OASIS Localization Interchange File Format. It
replaces the 1.2 element soup with an explicit `<unit>`/`<segment>`
structure, file-scoped notes, a small set of inline tag elements
(`ph`, `pc`, `sc`/`ec`, `mrk`), and per-segment translation states. The
reader accepts the 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 namespaces as one compatible
family.

The reader emits one block per translatable unit and preserves inline
markup as raw inner XML so unmodified content round-trips byte-for-byte.
The writer can emit any 2.x version: by default it preserves the input
document's version on round-trip and falls back to 2.2 for content
extracted from a non-XLIFF source. This mirrors Okapi's XLIFF 2 filter.

codeFinderRules.description: Regular-expression patterns that match inline codes in content.
codeFinderRules.help:
A list of regular expressions; each match within translatable text is
protected as an inline code rather than translated. Only takes effect
when `useCodeFinder` is enabled; invalid patterns are skipped.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for the rules to take effect

discardInvalidTargets.description: Discard targets that fail validation instead of rejecting the file.
discardInvalidTargets.help:
When enabled, a `<target>` that fails validation is dropped while the
rest of the file is still processed. When disabled (the default), an
invalid target rejects the file. Enable it to salvage usable content
from partially invalid input.

forceUniqueIds.description: Ensure inline tag ids are unique within each unit.
forceUniqueIds.help:
When enabled, inline tag `id` values are rewritten so each is unique
within its `<unit>`. When disabled (the default), ids are left as-is.
Enable it for inputs whose inline ids may collide and where strict
validators require per-unit uniqueness.

ignoreTagTypeMatch.description: Ignore tag-type mismatches between source and target segments.
ignoreTagTypeMatch.help:
When enabled, a mismatch between the inline tag types in the source
and the target segment is tolerated rather than treated as an error.
When disabled (the default), the tag types are expected to match.

useCodeFinder.description: Detect inline codes inside content using regular expressions.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes in translatable text,
using the patterns in `codeFinderRules`. This supplements the native
XLIFF 2 inline tag elements. When disabled (the default), only the
native inline elements are recognised.

version.help:
Selects the output XLIFF version. Leave it empty (the default) to
preserve the input document's version on round-trip and to fall back
to 2.2 for content with no source version. Set `2.0`, `2.1`, or `2.2`
to force a specific version; 2.0 and 2.1 share the
`urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:2.0` namespace, while 2.2
introduces `urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:2.2`.

writeOriginalData.description: Include original data in the output when available.
writeOriginalData.help:
When enabled (the default), the `<originalData>` carried by inline
tags is written to the output, preserving the original markup that
placeholders stand in for. When disabled, original data is omitted
from the output.

processingNotes: One block is emitted per translatable unit; inline markup is preserved as raw inner XML for byte-faithful round-trips.
The 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 namespaces are accepted as a compatible family on read.

examples.title: Force XLIFF 2.0 output
examples.description: Emit 2.0 regardless of the input document version.

examples.title: Tolerate invalid targets
examples.description: Drop targets that fail validation rather than rejecting the file.

entries.description: Extract and reinsert translatable text from generic XML, controlled by element and attribute rules.

schema.title: XML Format
schema.description: Configuration for the generic XML format reader/writer

attributes.title: Attribute rules
attributes.description: Advanced attribute-specific processing rules with element scope constraints

blockTypeMap.title: Block type map
blockTypeMap.description: Map of element names to block type strings for semantic type annotation

codeFinderRules.title: Code finder rules
codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within translatable text

elements.title: Element rules
elements.description: Advanced element-specific processing rules with conditions, inline marking, and translatable attribute mappings

excludeByDefault.title: Exclude by default
excludeByDefault.description: Exclude all elements unless explicitly included by an element rule with INCLUDE

excludedElements.title: Excluded elements
excludedElements.description: Element names whose content is excluded from extraction

groupElements.title: Group elements
groupElements.description: Element names that produce group/layer boundaries in the output

idAttributes.title: ID attributes
idAttributes.description: Attribute names used to extract block IDs from elements

inlineElements.title: Inline elements
inlineElements.description: Element names treated as inline (spans within text) rather than block-level

preserveWhitespace.title: Preserve whitespace
preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve original whitespace in text content instead of collapsing it

preserveWhitespaceElements.title: Preserve whitespace elements
preserveWhitespaceElements.description: Element names that preserve whitespace regardless of the global setting

subfilters.title: Subfilters
subfilters.description: Array of {pattern, format} mappings for embedded content. Patterns use dot-separated element paths with glob support.

translatableAttributes.title: Translatable attributes
translatableAttributes.description: Attribute names that are translatable across all elements

translatableElements.title: Translatable elements
translatableElements.description: Element names whose text content is translatable. If empty, all text content is translatable.

useCodeFinder.title: Enable inline code detection
useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based detection of inline codes within translatable text

doc.overview:
The **XML** format reads generic XML documents, extracts the text of
configured elements and attributes as translatable blocks, and writes the
translations back while preserving the surrounding markup. Inline elements
become inline codes within a block, so formatting tags survive translation.

Extraction is rule-driven. The simple lists — `translatableElements`,
`translatableAttributes`, `inlineElements`, `excludedElements` — cover most
cases. The advanced `elements` and `attributes` maps express richer,
ITS-style rules with attribute conditions, ID and translatable-attribute
mappings, and explicit include/exclude behaviour. When no translatable
elements are listed, all text content is treated as translatable; set
`excludeByDefault` to invert that and include only what rules opt in.

attributes.help:
A map of attribute name (or a single-quoted regex pattern) to a rule
object. A rule may carry `ruleTypes` (such as `ATTRIBUTE_TRANS`,
`ATTRIBUTE_ID`, `ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE`) and an element scope via
`onlyTheseElements` or `allElementsExcept`.
attributes.values: Map of attribute name → rule object (default empty)

blockTypeMap.description: Map element names to semantic block-type strings.
blockTypeMap.help:
A map from element name to a block-type string, annotating the resulting
blocks with a semantic type for downstream tools.
blockTypeMap.values: Map of element name → type string (default empty)

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within text.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

elements.help:
A map of element name (or a single-quoted regex pattern) to a rule
object. A rule may carry `ruleTypes` (`TEXTUNIT`, `INLINE`, `EXCLUDE`,
`INCLUDE`, `PRESERVE_WHITESPACE`, …), an attribute `conditions` triplet,
`idAttributes`, and a `translatableAttributes` map. Conditions let a rule
fire only when an attribute matches, mirroring ITS-style selectors.
elements.values: Map of element name → rule object (default empty)

excludeByDefault.description: Exclude all elements unless a rule includes them.
excludeByDefault.help:
When enabled, all elements are excluded from extraction unless an element
rule explicitly includes them with an `INCLUDE` rule type. This inverts
the default, where text content is translatable unless excluded.

excludedElements.description: Element names whose content is excluded.
excludedElements.help:
A list of element names whose content is not extracted, even when their
text would otherwise be translatable.
examples: `[script, style]` — never extract the content of these elements

groupElements.description: Element names that produce group/layer boundaries.
groupElements.help:
A list of element names that create group (layer) boundaries in the
output, grouping the blocks they contain.

idAttributes.description: Attribute names used to derive block IDs.
idAttributes.help:
A list of attribute names consulted, in order, to obtain a block's ID
from the enclosing element. When none match, the `id` attribute is used
as a fallback.
examples: `[name, key]` — use a `name` or `key` attribute as the block ID

inlineElements.description: Element names treated as inline spans.
inlineElements.help:
A list of element names treated as inline — represented as inline codes
within the surrounding text block rather than as block boundaries.
examples: `[b, i, span]` — treat these as inline formatting within text

preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve whitespace globally instead of collapsing it.
preserveWhitespace.help:
When enabled, whitespace in text content is preserved as-is. By default
runs of whitespace are collapsed to a single space and trimmed.

preserveWhitespaceElements.description: Element names that always preserve whitespace.
preserveWhitespaceElements.help:
A list of element names whose text content keeps its original whitespace
regardless of the global `preserveWhitespace` setting.
examples: `[pre, code]` — preserve whitespace inside these elements

subfilters.description: Per-pattern subfilter mappings for embedded content.
subfilters.help:
An array of `{pattern, format}` objects routing the text content of
matching element paths to another format reader. Patterns use
dot-separated element paths with glob support. Each mapping requires a
non-empty `pattern` and `format`.
examples: `[{pattern: 'root.body', format: html}]` — parse `body` content as HTML

translatableAttributes.description: Attribute names that are translatable across all elements.
translatableAttributes.help:
A list of attribute names whose values are extracted as translatable,
on any element that carries them.

translatableElements.description: Element names whose text content is translatable.
translatableElements.help:
A list of element names whose text content is extracted as translatable.
When empty, all text content is treated as translatable.
translatableElements.values: Array of element names (default empty — all text translatable)
examples: `[title, para]` — extract the text of `title` and `para` elements

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes within translatable text.
The patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

limitations: This is a generic XML reader; for specific XML dialects (such as RESX) start from a tailored rule set rather than the bare defaults.
Element and attribute names in advanced rules wrapped in single quotes are treated as anchored regular expressions.
processingNotes: Inline elements become inline codes within blocks; block-level elements form the surrounding structure.
Element rules with `INCLUDE`/`EXCLUDE` combine with `excludeByDefault` to give fine control over which content is extracted.

examples.title: Extract specific elements
examples.description: Translate only title and para text, treating b and i as inline.
examples.config:
translatableElements:
  - title
  - para
inlineElements:
  - b
  - i

examples.title: Translatable attributes
examples.description: Extract title and alt attribute values across all elements.

examples.title: Conditional extraction with rules
examples.description: Include only div elements whose translate attribute is yes.
examples.config:
excludeByDefault: true
elements:
  div:
    ruleTypes:
      - INCLUDE
    conditions:
      - translate
      - EQUALS
      - "yes"

doc.overview:
The **YAML** format reads YAML documents, extracts scalar string values as
translatable blocks, and writes the translations back while preserving the
surrounding structure, comments, and ordering. It suits Rails locale files,
application configuration, and other key/value catalogues expressed in YAML.

By default only string scalars are extracted. You can also extract
non-string scalars, restrict extraction to specific key paths with
glob-style patterns, detect inline codes, and route values containing
embedded markup (such as HTML) to a subfilter.

codeFinderRules.description: Regex patterns that match inline codes within values.
codeFinderRules.help:
An array of regular expressions; text matching any of them is treated
as an inline code rather than translatable words. Only used when
`useCodeFinder` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for these patterns to apply

extractNonStrings.help:
When enabled, non-string scalars — booleans, numbers, and nulls — are
also extracted as translatable blocks. By default only string scalars
are extracted.

keyPathPatterns.description: Glob patterns selecting which key paths are extracted.
keyPathPatterns.help:
An array of glob-style key-path patterns. When non-empty, only keys
matching one of the patterns are extracted. `*` matches a single key
segment; `**` matches any number of segments at any depth.
keyPathPatterns.values: Array of glob patterns (default empty — all keys extracted)
examples: `en.**` — extract only keys nested under the top-level `en` key
`*.title` — extract the `title` key directly under any top-level key

subfilter.description: Subfilter format applied to scalar values.
subfilter.help:
The name of a format used as a subfilter (for example `html`) to parse
embedded markup inside extracted scalar values, so the markup is exposed
as inline codes rather than translated as literal text.
examples: `html` — parse HTML inside YAML scalar values

useCodeFinder.description: Enable regex-based inline-code detection.
useCodeFinder.help:
Enables regex-based detection of inline codes within string values. The
patterns are supplied via `codeFinderRules`.

limitations: Only scalar values are translatable; mapping keys and sequence structure form the non-translatable skeleton.
processingNotes: Document structure, key order, and comments are preserved across the read/write roundtrip.

examples.description: Translate only the keys under the en branch of a Rails-style locale file.

examples.description: Parse HTML markup within YAML scalar values.
